result:
The old lighthouse stood at the edge of the cliff, its white paint peeling like ancient skin. For seventy years, Thomas had tended the flame that guided ships through the treacherous waters below. Every evening, as the sun dissolved into the horizon, he would climb the spiral staircase, his calloused hands gripping the cold iron railing, and ignite the great lens that turned darkness into salvation.

Tonight would be his last. The coast guard had decided to automate the lighthouse, a decision made by men who had never felt the salt wind tear at their faces or heard the desperate cries of sailors in the storm. They called it progress. Thomas called it the end of an era.

He climbed anyway. One final time. The lens still sparkled as it had on his first night, when his father had stood beside him, teaching him the ancient rhythm of light and shadow. "The sea remembers those who respect it," his father had said. "It forgets no one."

Thomas lit the flame and watched it dance. In the distance, a ship's horn sounded, a low mournful greeting. He imagined the passengers aboard, unaware of the man in the lighthouse, unaware of the generations of keepers who had kept their journey safe.

As he descended for the last time, Thomas smiled. The light would continue to shine, even without him. And that, he realized, was enough. The sea would keep its secrets, and the lighthouse would keep its watch, long after the last keeper walked away.

As he descended for the last time, Thomas smiled. The light would continue to shine, even without him. The sea would keep its secrets, and the lighthouse would keep its watch.
